77: // GenericStore interface can be used for type assertions when we need to access the underlying strategies.
78: type GenericStore interface {
79: 	GetCreateStrategy() rest.RESTCreateStrategy
80: 	GetUpdateStrategy() rest.RESTUpdateStrategy
81: 	GetDeleteStrategy() rest.RESTDeleteStrategy
82: }
83: 
84: // Store implements k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/rest.StandardStorage. It's
85: // intended to be embeddable and allows the consumer to implement any
86: // non-generic functions that are required. This object is intended to be
87: // copyable so that it can be used in different ways but share the same
88: // underlying behavior.
89: //
90: // All fields are required unless specified.
91: //
92: // The intended use of this type is embedding within a Kind specific
93: // RESTStorage implementation. This type provides CRUD semantics on a Kubelike
94: // resource, handling details like conflict detection with ResourceVersion and
95: // semantics. The RESTCreateStrategy, RESTUpdateStrategy, and
96: // RESTDeleteStrategy are generic across all backends, and encapsulate logic
97: // specific to the API.
98: //
99: // TODO: make the default exposed methods exactly match a generic RESTStorage
100: type Store struct {
101: 	// NewFunc returns a new instance of the type this registry returns for a
102: 	// GET of a single object, e.g.:
103: 	//
104: 	// curl GET /apis/group/version/namespaces/my-ns/myresource/name-of-object
105: 	NewFunc func() runtime.Object
106: 
107: 	// NewListFunc returns a new list of the type this registry; it is the
108: 	// type returned when the resource is listed, e.g.:
109: 	//
110: 	// curl GET /apis/group/version/namespaces/my-ns/myresource
111: 	NewListFunc func() runtime.Object
112: 
113: 	// DefaultQualifiedResource is the pluralized name of the resource.
114: 	// This field is used if there is no request info present in the context.
115: 	// See qualifiedResourceFromContext for details.
116: 	DefaultQualifiedResource schema.GroupResource
117: 
118: 	// SingularQualifiedResource is the singular name of the resource.
119: 	SingularQualifiedResource schema.GroupResource
120: 
121: 	// KeyRootFunc returns the root etcd key for this resource; should not
122: 	// include trailing "/".  This is used for operations that work on the
123: 	// entire collection (listing and watching).
124: 	//
125: 	// KeyRootFunc and KeyFunc must be supplied together or not at all.
126: 	KeyRootFunc func(ctx context.Context) string
127: 
128: 	// KeyFunc returns the key for a specific object in the collection.
129: 	// KeyFunc is called for Create/Update/Get/Delete. Note that 'namespace'
130: 	// can be gotten from ctx.
131: 	//
132: 	// KeyFunc and KeyRootFunc must be supplied together or not at all.
133: 	KeyFunc func(ctx context.Context, name string) (string, error)
134: 
135: 	// ObjectNameFunc returns the name of an object or an error.
136: 	ObjectNameFunc func(obj runtime.Object) (string, error)
137: 
138: 	// TTLFunc returns the TTL (time to live) that objects should be persisted
139: 	// with. The existing parameter is the current TTL or the default for this
140: 	// operation. The update parameter indicates whether this is an operation
141: 	// against an existing object.
142: 	//
143: 	// Objects that are persisted with a TTL are evicted once the TTL expires.
144: 	TTLFunc func(obj runtime.Object, existing uint64, update bool) (uint64, error)
145: 
146: 	// PredicateFunc returns a matcher corresponding to the provided labels
147: 	// and fields. The SelectionPredicate returned should return true if the
148: 	// object matches the given field and label selectors.
149: 	PredicateFunc func(label labels.Selector, field fields.Selector) storage.SelectionPredicate
150:

62: type APIInstaller struct {
63: 	group             *APIGroupVersion
64: 	prefix            string // Path prefix where API resources are to be registered.
65: 	minRequestTimeout time.Duration
66: }
67: 
68: // Struct capturing information about an action (MethodGet, MethodPost, MethodWatch, MethodProxy, etc).
69: type action struct {
70: 	Verb          string               // Verb identifying the action (MethodGet, MethodPost, MethodWatch, MethodProxy, etc).
71: 	Path          string               // The path of the action
72: 	Params        []*restful.Parameter // List of parameters associated with the action.
73: 	Namer         handlers.ScopeNamer
74: 	AllNamespaces bool // true iff the action is namespaced but works on aggregate result for all namespaces
75: }
76: 
77: func ConvertGroupVersionIntoToDiscovery(list []metav1.APIResource) ([]apidiscoveryv2.APIResourceDiscovery, error) {
78: 	var apiResourceList []apidiscoveryv2.APIResourceDiscovery
79: 	parentResources := make(map[string]int)
80: 
81: 	// Loop through all top-level resources
82: 	for _, r := range list {
83: 		if strings.Contains(r.Name, "/") {
84: 			// Skip subresources for now so we can get the list of resources
85: 			continue
86: 		}
87: 
88: 		var scope apidiscoveryv2.ResourceScope
89: 		if r.Namespaced {
90: 			scope = apidiscoveryv2.ScopeNamespace
91: 		} else {
92: 			scope = apidiscoveryv2.ScopeCluster
93: 		}
94: 
95: 		resource := apidiscoveryv2.APIResourceDiscovery{
96: 			Resource: r.Name,
97: 			Scope:    scope,
98: 			ResponseKind: &metav1.GroupVersionKind{
99: 				Group:   r.Group,
100: 				Version: r.Version,
101: 				Kind:    r.Kind,
102: 			},
103: 			Verbs:            r.Verbs,
104: 			ShortNames:       r.ShortNames,
105: 			Categories:       r.Categories,
106: 			SingularResource: r.SingularName,
107: 		}
108: 		apiResourceList = append(apiResourceList, resource)
109: 		parentResources[r.Name] = len(apiResourceList) - 1
110: 	}
111: 
112: 	// Loop through all subresources
113: 	for _, r := range list {
114: 		// Split resource name and subresource name
115: 		split := strings.SplitN(r.Name, "/", 2)
116: 
117: 		if len(split) != 2 {
118: 			// Skip parent resources
119: 			continue
120: 		}

273: 	}
274: 	return key

291: 	}

294: }

17: package handlers
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"bytes"
21: 	"context"
22: 	"fmt"
23: 	"net/http"

31: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
32: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/meta"
33: 	metainternalversionscheme "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/internalversion/scheme"
34: 	metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"

37: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/schema"
38: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission"

41: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/finisher"
42: 	requestmetrics "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/metrics"
43: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/negotiation"
44: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/request"
45: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/rest"
46: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/dryrun"

51: var namespaceGVR = schema.GroupVersionResource{Group: "", Version: "v1", Resource: "namespaces"}
52: 
53: func createHandler(r rest.NamedCreater, scope *RequestScope, admit admission.Interface, includeName bool) http.HandlerFunc {
54: 	return func(w http.ResponseWriter, req *http.Request) {
55: 		ctx := req.Context()
56: 		// For performance tracking purposes.
57: 		ctx, span := tracing.Start(ctx, "Create", traceFields(req)...)
58: 		req = req.WithContext(ctx)
59: 		defer span.End(500 * time.Millisecond)
60: 
61: 		namespace, name, err := scope.Namer.Name(req)
62: 		if err != nil {
63: 			if includeName {
64: 				// name was required, return
65: 				scope.err(err, w, req)
66: 				return
67: 			}
68: 
69: 			// otherwise attempt to look up the namespace
70: 			namespace, err = scope.Namer.Namespace(req)
71: 			if err != nil {
72: 				scope.err(err, w, req)
73: 				return
74: 			}
75: 		}
76: 
77: 		// enforce a timeout of at most requestTimeoutUpperBound (34s) or less if the user-provided
78: 		// timeout inside the parent context is lower than requestTimeoutUpperBound.
79: 		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, requestTimeoutUpperBound)
80: 		defer cancel()
81: 		outputMediaType, _, err := negotiation.NegotiateOutputMediaType(req, scope.Serializer, scope)
82: 		if err != nil {
83: 			scope.err(err, w, req)
84: 			return
85: 		}
86: 
87: 		gv := scope.Kind.GroupVersion()
88: 		s, err := negotiation.NegotiateInputSerializer(req, false, scope.Serializer)
89: 		if err != nil {
90: 			scope.err(err, w, req)
91: 			return
92: 		}
93: 
94: 		body, err := limitedReadBodyWithRecordMetric(ctx, req, scope.MaxRequestBodyBytes, scope.Resource.GroupResource(), requestmetrics.Create)
95: 		if err != nil {
96: 			span.AddEvent("limitedReadBody failed", attribute.Int("len", len(body)), attribute.String("err", err.Error()))
97: 			scope.err(err, w, req)
98: 			return
99: 		}
100: 		span.AddEvent("limitedReadBody succeeded", attribute.Int("len", len(body)))

It should include the full filter\n44: \t// chain and then call the Director.\n45: \tFullHandlerChain http.Handler\n46: \t// The registered APIs.  InstallAPIs uses this.  Other servers probably shouldn't access this directly.\n47: \tGoRestfulContainer *restful.Container\n48: \t// NonGoRestfulMux is the final HTTP handler in the chain.\n49: \t// It comes after all filters and the API handling\n50: \t// This is where other servers can attach handler to various parts of the chain.\n51: \tNonGoRestfulMux *mux.PathRecorderMux\n52: \n53: \t// Director is here so that we can properly handle fall through and proxy cases.\n54: \t// This looks a bit bonkers, but here's what's happening.  We need to have /apis handling registered in gorestful in order to have\n55: \t// swagger generated for compatibility.  Doing that with `/apis` as a webservice, means that it forcibly 404s (no defaulting allowed)\n56: \t// all requests which are not /apis or /apis/.  We need those calls to fall through behind gorestful for proper delegation.  Trying to\n57: \t// register for a pattern which includes everything behind it doesn't work because gorestful negotiates for verbs and content encoding\n58: \t// and all those things go crazy when gorestful really just needs to pass through.  In addition, openapi enforces unique verb constraints\n59: \t// which we don't fit into and it still muddies up swagger.  Trying to switch the webservices into a route doesn't work because the\n60: \t//  containing webservice faces all the same problems listed above.\n61: \t// This leads to the crazy thing done here.  Our mux does what we need, so we'll place it in front of gorestful.  It will introspect to\n62: \t// decide if the route is likely to be handled by gorestful and route there if needed.  Otherwise, it goes to NonGoRestfulMux mux in\n63: \t// order to handle \"normal\" paths and delegation. Hopefully no API consumers will ever have to deal with this level of detail.  I think\n64: \t// we should consider completely removing gorestful.\n65: \t// Other servers should only use this opaquely to delegate to an API server.\n66: \tDirector http.Handler\n67: }\n68: \n69: // HandlerChainBuilderFn is used to wrap the GoRestfulContainer handler using the provided handler chain.\n70: // It is normally used to apply filtering like authentication and authorization\n71: type HandlerChainBuilderFn func(apiHandler http.Handler) http.Handler\n72: \n73: func NewAPIServerHandler(name string, s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, handlerChainBuilder HandlerChainBuilderFn, notFoundHandler http.Handler) *APIServerHandler {\n74: \tnonGoRestfulMux := mux.NewPathRecorderMux(name)\n75: \tif notFoundHandler != nil {\n76: \t\tnonGoRestfulMux.NotFoundHandler(notFoundHandler)\n77: \t}\n78: \n79: \tgorestfulContainer := restful.NewContainer()\n80: \tgorestfulContainer.Router(restful.CurlyRouter{}) // e.g. for proxy/{kind}/{name}/{*}\n81: \tgorestfulContainer.RecoverHandler(func(panicReason interface{}, httpWriter http.ResponseWriter) {\n82: \t\tlogStackOnRecover(s, panicReason, httpWriter)\n83: \t})\n84: \tgorestfulContainer.ServiceErrorHandler(func(serviceErr restful.ServiceError, request *restful.Request, response *restful.Response) {\n85: \t\tserviceErrorHandler(s, serviceErr, request, response)\n86: \t})\n87: \n88: \tdirector := director{\n89: \t\tname:               name,\n90: \t\tgoRestfulContainer: gorestfulContainer,\n91: \t\tnonGoRestfulMux:    nonGoRestfulMux,\n92: \t}\n93: \n94: \treturn \u0026APIServerHandler{\n95: \t\tFullHandlerChain:   handlerChainBuilder(director),\n96: \t\tGoRestfulContainer: gorestfulContainer,\n97: \t\tNonGoRestfulMux:    nonGoRestfulMux,\n98: \t\tDirector:           director,\n99: \t}\n100: }\n101: \n102: // ListedPaths returns the paths that should be shown under /\n103: func (a *APIServerHandler) ListedPaths() []string {\n104: \tvar handledPaths []string\n105: \t// Extract the paths handled using restful.WebService\n106: \tfor _, ws := range a.GoRestfulContainer.RegisteredWebServices() {\n107: \t\thandledPaths = append(handledPaths, ws.RootPath())\n108: \t}\n109: \thandledPaths = append(handledPaths, a.NonGoRestfulMux.ListedPaths()...)\n110: \tsort.Strings(handledPaths)\n111: \n112: \treturn handledPaths\n113: }\n114: \n115: type director struct {\n116: \tname               string\n117: \tgoRestfulContainer *restful.Container\n118: \tnonGoRestfulMux    *mux.PathRecorderMux\n119: }\n120: \n121: func (d director) ServeHTTP(w http.ResponseWriter, req *http.Request) {\n122: \tpath := req.URL.Path\n123: \n124: \t// check to see if our webservices want to claim this path\n125: \tfor _, ws := range d.goRestfulContainer.RegisteredWebServices() {\n126: \t\tswitch {\n127: \t\tcase ws.RootPath() == \"/apis\":\n128: \t\t\t// if we are exactly /apis or /apis/, then we need special handling in loop.\n129: \t\t\t// normally these are passed to the nonGoRestfulMux, but if discovery is enabled, it will go directly.\n130: \t\t\t// We can't rely on a prefix match since /apis matches everything (see the big comment on Director above)\n131: \t\t\tif path == \"/apis\" || path == \"/apis/\" {\n132: \t\t\t\tklog.V(5).Infof(\"%v: %v %q satisfied by gorestful with webservice %v\", d.name, req.Method, path, ws.RootPath())\n133: \t\t\t\t// don't use servemux here because gorestful servemuxes get messed up when removing webservices\n134: \t\t\t\t// TODO fix gorestful, remove TPRs, or stop using gorestful\n135: \t\t\t\td.goRestfulContainer.Dispatch(w, req)\n136: \t\t\t\treturn\n137: \t\t\t}\n138: \n139: \t\tcase strings.HasPrefix(path, ws.RootPath()):\n140: \t\t\t// ensure an exact match or a path boundary match\n141: \t\t\tif len(path) == len(ws.RootPath()) || path[len(ws.RootPath())] == '/' {\n142: \t\t\t\tklog.V(5).Infof(\"%v: %v %q satisfied by gorestful with webservice %v\", d.name, req.Method, path, ws.RootPath())\n143: \t\t\t\t// don't use servemux here because gorestful servemuxes get messed up when removing webservices\n144: \t\t\t\t// TODO fix gorestful, remove TPRs, or stop using gorestful\n145: \t\t\t\td.goRestfulContainer.Dispatch(w, req)\n146: \t\t\t\treturn\n147: \t\t\t}\n148: \t\t}\n149: \t}\n150: \n151: \t// if we didn't find a match, then we just skip gorestful altogether\n152: \tklog.V(5).Infof(\"%v: %v %q satisfied by nonGoRestful\", d.name, req.Method, path)\n153: \td.nonGoRestfulMux.ServeHTTP(w, req)\n154: }\n155: \n156: // TODO: Unify with RecoverPanics?\n157: func logStackOnRecover(s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, panicReason interface{}, w http.ResponseWriter) {\n158: \tvar buffer bytes.Buffer\n159: \tbuffer.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"recover from panic situation: - %v\\r\\n\", panicReason))\n160: \tfor i := 2; ; i++ {\n161: \t\t_, file, line, ok := rt.Caller(i)\n162: \t\tif !ok {\n163: \t\t\tbreak\n164: \t\t}\n165: \t\tbuffer.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf(\"    %s:%d\\r\\n\", file, line))\n166: \t}\n167: \tklog.Errorln(buffer.String())\n168: \n169: \theaders := http.Header{}\n170: \tif ct := w.Header().Get(\"Content-Type\"); len(ct) \u003e 0 {\n171: \t\theaders.Set(\"Accept\", ct)\n172: \t}\n173: \tresponsewriters.ErrorNegotiated(apierrors.NewGenericServerResponse(http.StatusInternalServerError, \"\", schema.GroupResource{}, \"\", \"\", 0, false), s, schema.GroupVersion{}, w, \u0026http.Request{Header: headers})\n174: }\n175: \n176: func serviceErrorHandler(s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, serviceErr restful.ServiceError, request *restful.Request, resp *restful.Response) {\n177: \tresponsewriters.ErrorNegotiated(\n178: \t\tapierrors.NewGenericServerResponse(serviceErr.Code, \"\", schema.GroupResource{}, \"\", serviceErr.Message, 0, false),\n179: \t\ts,\n180: \t\tschema.GroupVersion{},\n181: \t\tresp,\n182: \t\trequest.Request,\n183: \t)\n184: }\n185: \n186: // ServeHTTP makes it an http.Handler\n187: func (a *APIServerHandler) ServeHTTP(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {\n188: \ta.FullHandlerChain.ServeHTTP(w, r)\n189: }\n190: 17: package server
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"bytes"
21: 	"fmt"
22: 	"net/http"
23: 	rt "runtime"
24: 	"sort"
25: 	"strings"
26: 
27: 	"github.com/emicklei/go-restful/v3"
28: 	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
29: 
30: 	apierrors "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
31: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
32: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/schema"
33: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/responsewriters"
34: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/mux"
35: )
36: 
37: // APIServerHandlers holds the different http.Handlers used by the API server.
38: // This includes the full handler chain, the director (which chooses between gorestful and nonGoRestful,
39: // the gorestful handler (used for the API) which falls through to the nonGoRestful handler on unregistered paths,
40: // and the nonGoRestful handler (which can contain a fallthrough of its own)
41: // FullHandlerChain -> Director -> {GoRestfulContainer,NonGoRestfulMux} based on inspection of registered web services
42: type APIServerHandler struct {
43: 	// FullHandlerChain is the one that is eventually served with.  It should include the full filter
44: 	// chain and then call the Director.
45: 	FullHandlerChain http.Handler
46: 	// The registered APIs.  InstallAPIs uses this.  Other servers probably shouldn't access this directly.
47: 	GoRestfulContainer *restful.Container
48: 	// NonGoRestfulMux is the final HTTP handler in the chain.
49: 	// It comes after all filters and the API handling
50: 	// This is where other servers can attach handler to various parts of the chain.
51: 	NonGoRestfulMux *mux.PathRecorderMux
52: 
53: 	// Director is here so that we can properly handle fall through and proxy cases.
54: 	// This looks a bit bonkers, but here's what's happening.  We need to have /apis handling registered in gorestful in order to have
55: 	// swagger generated for compatibility.  Doing that with `/apis` as a webservice, means that it forcibly 404s (no defaulting allowed)
56: 	// all requests which are not /apis or /apis/.  We need those calls to fall through behind gorestful for proper delegation.  Trying to
57: 	// register for a pattern which includes everything behind it doesn't work because gorestful negotiates for verbs and content encoding
58: 	// and all those things go crazy when gorestful really just needs to pass through.  In addition, openapi enforces unique verb constraints
59: 	// which we don't fit into and it still muddies up swagger.  Trying to switch the webservices into a route doesn't work because the
60: 	//  containing webservice faces all the same problems listed above.
61: 	// This leads to the crazy thing done here.  Our mux does what we need, so we'll place it in front of gorestful.  It will introspect to
62: 	// decide if the route is likely to be handled by gorestful and route there if needed.  Otherwise, it goes to NonGoRestfulMux mux in
63: 	// order to handle "normal" paths and delegation. Hopefully no API consumers will ever have to deal with this level of detail.  I think
64: 	// we should consider completely removing gorestful.
65: 	// Other servers should only use this opaquely to delegate to an API server.
66: 	Director http.Handler
67: }
68: 
69: // HandlerChainBuilderFn is used to wrap the GoRestfulContainer handler using the provided handler chain.
70: // It is normally used to apply filtering like authentication and authorization
71: type HandlerChainBuilderFn func(apiHandler http.Handler) http.Handler
72: 
73: func NewAPIServerHandler(name string, s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, handlerChainBuilder HandlerChainBuilderFn, notFoundHandler http.Handler) *APIServerHandler {
74: 	nonGoRestfulMux := mux.NewPathRecorderMux(name)
75: 	if notFoundHandler != nil {
76: 		nonGoRestfulMux.NotFoundHandler(notFoundHandler)
77: 	}
78: 
79: 	gorestfulContainer := restful.NewContainer()
80: 	gorestfulContainer.Router(restful.CurlyRouter{}) // e.g. for proxy/{kind}/{name}/{*}
81: 	gorestfulContainer.RecoverHandler(func(panicReason interface{}, httpWriter http.ResponseWriter) {
82: 		logStackOnRecover(s, panicReason, httpWriter)
83: 	})
84: 	gorestfulContainer.ServiceErrorHandler(func(serviceErr restful.ServiceError, request *restful.Request, response *restful.Response) {
85: 		serviceErrorHandler(s, serviceErr, request, response)
86: 	})
87: 
88: 	director := director{
89: 		name:               name,
90: 		goRestfulContainer: gorestfulContainer,
91: 		nonGoRestfulMux:    nonGoRestfulMux,
92: 	}
93: 
94: 	return &APIServerHandler{
95: 		FullHandlerChain:   handlerChainBuilder(director),
96: 		GoRestfulContainer: gorestfulContainer,
97: 		NonGoRestfulMux:    nonGoRestfulMux,
98: 		Director:           director,
99: 	}
100: }
101: 
102: // ListedPaths returns the paths that should be shown under /
103: func (a *APIServerHandler) ListedPaths() []string {
104: 	var handledPaths []string
105: 	// Extract the paths handled using restful.WebService
106: 	for _, ws := range a.GoRestfulContainer.RegisteredWebServices() {
107: 		handledPaths = append(handledPaths, ws.RootPath())
108: 	}
109: 	handledPaths = append(handledPaths, a.NonGoRestfulMux.ListedPaths()...)
110: 	sort.Strings(handledPaths)
111: 
112: 	return handledPaths
113: }
114: 
115: type director struct {
116: 	name               string
117: 	goRestfulContainer *restful.Container
118: 	nonGoRestfulMux    *mux.PathRecorderMux
119: }
120: 
121: func (d director) ServeHTTP(w http.ResponseWriter, req *http.Request) {
122: 	path := req.URL.Path
123: 
124: 	// check to see if our webservices want to claim this path
125: 	for _, ws := range d.goRestfulContainer.RegisteredWebServices() {
126: 		switch {
127: 		case ws.RootPath() == "/apis":
128: 			// if we are exactly /apis or /apis/, then we need special handling in loop.
129: 			// normally these are passed to the nonGoRestfulMux, but if discovery is enabled, it will go directly.
130: 			// We can't rely on a prefix match since /apis matches everything (see the big comment on Director above)
131: 			if path == "/apis" || path == "/apis/" {
132: 				klog.V(5).Infof("%v: %v %q satisfied by gorestful with webservice %v", d.name, req.Method, path, ws.RootPath())
133: 				// don't use servemux here because gorestful servemuxes get messed up when removing webservices
134: 				// TODO fix gorestful, remove TPRs, or stop using gorestful
135: 				d.goRestfulContainer.Dispatch(w, req)
136: 				return
137: 			}
138: 
139: 		case strings.HasPrefix(path, ws.RootPath()):
140: 			// ensure an exact match or a path boundary match
141: 			if len(path) == len(ws.RootPath()) || path[len(ws.RootPath())] == '/' {
142: 				klog.V(5).Infof("%v: %v %q satisfied by gorestful with webservice %v", d.name, req.Method, path, ws.RootPath())
143: 				// don't use servemux here because gorestful servemuxes get messed up when removing webservices
144: 				// TODO fix gorestful, remove TPRs, or stop using gorestful
145: 				d.goRestfulContainer.Dispatch(w, req)
146: 				return
147: 			}
148: 		}
149: 	}
150: 
151: 	// if we didn't find a match, then we just skip gorestful altogether
152: 	klog.V(5).Infof("%v: %v %q satisfied by nonGoRestful", d.name, req.Method, path)
153: 	d.nonGoRestfulMux.ServeHTTP(w, req)
154: }
155: 
156: // TODO: Unify with RecoverPanics?
157: func logStackOnRecover(s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, panicReason interface{}, w http.ResponseWriter) {
158: 	var buffer bytes.Buffer
159: 	buffer.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("recover from panic situation: - %v\r\n", panicReason))
160: 	for i := 2; ; i++ {
161: 		_, file, line, ok := rt.Caller(i)
162: 		if !ok {
163: 			break
164: 		}
165: 		buffer.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("    %s:%d\r\n", file, line))
166: 	}
167: 	klog.Errorln(buffer.String())
168: 
169: 	headers := http.Header{}
170: 	if ct := w.Header().Get("Content-Type"); len(ct) > 0 {
171: 		headers.Set("Accept", ct)
172: 	}
173: 	responsewriters.ErrorNegotiated(apierrors.NewGenericServerResponse(http.StatusInternalServerError, "", schema.GroupResource{}, "", "", 0, false), s, schema.GroupVersion{}, w, &http.Request{Header: headers})
174: }
175: 
176: func serviceErrorHandler(s runtime.NegotiatedSerializer, serviceErr restful.ServiceError, request *restful.Request, resp *restful.Response) {
177: 	responsewriters.ErrorNegotiated(
178: 		apierrors.NewGenericServerResponse(serviceErr.Code, "", schema.GroupResource{}, "", serviceErr.Message, 0, false),
179: 		s,
180: 		schema.GroupVersion{},
181: 		resp,
182: 		request.Request,
183: 	)
184: }
185: 
186: // ServeHTTP makes it an http.Handler
187: func (a *APIServerHandler) ServeHTTP(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
188: 	a.FullHandlerChain.ServeHTTP(w, r)
189: }

17: package server
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"sync"
21: )
22: 
23: /*
24: We make an attempt here to identify the events that take place during
25: lifecycle of the apiserver.
26: 
27: We also identify each event with a name so we can refer to it.
28: 
29: Events:
30: - ShutdownInitiated: KILL signal received
31: - AfterShutdownDelayDuration: shutdown delay duration has passed
32: - InFlightRequestsDrained: all in flight request(s) have been drained
33: - HasBeenReady is signaled when the readyz endpoint succeeds for the first time
34: 
35: The following is a sequence of shutdown events that we expect to see with
36:   'ShutdownSendRetryAfter' = false:
37: 
38: T0: ShutdownInitiated: KILL signal received
39: 	- /readyz starts returning red
40:     - run pre shutdown hooks
41: 
42: T0+70s: AfterShutdownDelayDuration: shutdown delay duration has passed
43: 	- the default value of 'ShutdownDelayDuration' is '70s'
44: 	- it's time to initiate shutdown of the HTTP Server, server.Shutdown is invoked
45: 	- as a consequene, the Close function has is called for all listeners
46:  	- the HTTP Server stops listening immediately
47: 	- any new request arriving on a new TCP socket is denied with
48:       a network error similar to 'connection refused'
49:     - the HTTP Server waits gracefully for existing requests to complete
50:       up to '60s' (dictated by ShutdownTimeout)
51: 	- active long running requests will receive a GOAWAY.
52: 
53: T0+70s: HTTPServerStoppedListening:
54: 	- this event is signaled when the HTTP Server has stopped listening
55:       which is immediately after server.Shutdown has been invoked
56: 
57: T0 + 70s + up-to 60s: InFlightRequestsDrained: existing in flight requests have been drained
58: 	- long running requests are outside of this scope
59: 	- up-to 60s: the default value of 'ShutdownTimeout' is 60s, this means that
60:       any request in flight has a hard timeout of 60s.
61: 	- it's time to call 'Shutdown' on the audit events since all
62: 	  in flight request(s) have drained.
63: 
64: 
65: The following is a sequence of shutdown events that we expect to see with
66:   'ShutdownSendRetryAfter' = true:
67: 
68: T0: ShutdownInitiated: KILL signal received
69: 	- /readyz starts returning red
70:     - run pre shutdown hooks
71: 
72: T0+70s: AfterShutdownDelayDuration: shutdown delay duration has passed
73: 	- the default value of 'ShutdownDelayDuration' is '70s'
74: 	- the HTTP Server will continue to listen
75: 	- the apiserver is not accepting new request(s)
76: 		- it includes new request(s) on a new or an existing TCP connection
77: 		- new request(s) arriving after this point are replied with a 429
78:       	  and the  response headers: 'Retry-After: 1` and 'Connection: close'
79: 	- note: these new request(s) will not show up in audit logs
80: 
81: T0 + 70s + up to 60s: InFlightRequestsDrained: existing in flight requests have been drained
82: 	- long running requests are outside of this scope
83: 	- up to 60s: the default value of 'ShutdownTimeout' is 60s, this means that
84:       any request in flight has a hard timeout of 60s.
85: 	- server.Shutdown is called, the HTTP Server stops listening immediately
86:     - the HTTP Server waits gracefully for existing requests to complete
87:       up to '2s' (it's hard coded right now)
88: */
89: 
90: // lifecycleSignal encapsulates a named apiserver event
91: type lifecycleSignal interface {
92: 	// Signal signals the event, indicating that the event has occurred.
93: 	// Signal is idempotent, once signaled the event stays signaled and
94: 	// it immediately unblocks any goroutine waiting for this event.
95: 	Signal()
96: 
97: 	// Signaled returns a channel that is closed when the underlying event
98: 	// has been signaled. Successive calls to Signaled return the same value.
99: 	Signaled() <-chan struct{}
100:

\t\"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime\"\n22: \t\"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission\"\n23: \t\"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server\"\n24: \t\"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/storagebackend\"\n25: \t\"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/feature\"\n26: \t\"k8s.io/client-go/dynamic\"\n27: \t\"k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes\"\n28: \t\"k8s.io/component-base/featuregate\"\n29: )\n30: \n31: // RecommendedOptions contains the recommended options for running an API server.\n32: // If you add something to this list, it should be in a logical grouping.\n33: // Each of them can be nil to leave the feature unconfigured on ApplyTo.\n34: type RecommendedOptions struct {\n35: \tEtcd           *EtcdOptions\n36: \tSecureServing  *SecureServingOptionsWithLoopback\n37: \tAuthentication *DelegatingAuthenticationOptions\n38: \tAuthorization  *DelegatingAuthorizationOptions\n39: \tAudit          *AuditOptions\n40: \tFeatures       *FeatureOptions\n41: \tCoreAPI        *CoreAPIOptions\n42: \n43: \t// FeatureGate is a way to plumb feature gate through if you have them.\n44: \tFeatureGate featuregate.FeatureGate\n45: \t// ExtraAdmissionInitializers is called once after all ApplyTo from the options above, to pass the returned\n46: \t// admission plugin initializers to Admission.ApplyTo.\n47: \tExtraAdmissionInitializers func(c *server.RecommendedConfig) 17: package options
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"github.com/spf13/pflag"
21: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
22: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission"
23: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server"
24: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/storagebackend"
25: 	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/feature"
26: 	"k8s.io/client-go/dynamic"
27: 	"k8s.io/client-go/kubernetes"
28: 	"k8s.io/component-base/featuregate"
29: )
30: 
31: // RecommendedOptions contains the recommended options for running an API server.
32: // If you add something to this list, it should be in a logical grouping.
33: // Each of them can be nil to leave the feature unconfigured on ApplyTo.
34: type RecommendedOptions struct {
35: 	Etcd           *EtcdOptions
36: 	SecureServing  *SecureServingOptionsWithLoopback
37: 	Authentication *DelegatingAuthenticationOptions
38: 	Authorization  *DelegatingAuthorizationOptions
39: 	Audit          *AuditOptions
40: 	Features       *FeatureOptions
41: 	CoreAPI        *CoreAPIOptions
42: 
43: 	// FeatureGate is a way to plumb feature gate through if you have them.
44: 	FeatureGate featuregate.FeatureGate
45: 	// ExtraAdmissionInitializers is called once after all ApplyTo from the options above, to pass the returned
46: 	// admission plugin initializers to Admission.ApplyTo.
47: 	ExtraAdmissionInitializers func(c *server.RecommendedConfig) ([]admission.PluginInitializer, error)
48: 	Admission                  *AdmissionOptions
49: 	// API Server Egress Selector is used to control outbound traffic from the API Server
50: 	EgressSelector *EgressSelectorOptions
51: 	// Traces contains options to control distributed request tracing.
52: 	Traces *TracingOptions
53: }
54: 
55: func NewRecommendedOptions(prefix string, codec runtime.Codec) *RecommendedOptions {
56: 	sso := NewSecureServingOptions()
57: 
58: 	// We are composing recommended options for an aggregated api-server,
59: 	// whose client is typically a proxy multiplexing many operations ---
60: 	// notably including long-running ones --- into one HTTP/2 connection
61: 	// into this server.  So allow many concurrent operations.
62: 	sso.HTTP2MaxStreamsPerConnection = 1000
63: 
64: 	return &RecommendedOptions{
65: 		Etcd:           NewEtcdOptions(storagebackend.NewDefaultConfig(prefix, codec)),
66: 		SecureServing:  sso.WithLoopback(),
67: 		Authentication: NewDelegatingAuthenticationOptions(),
68: 		Authorization:  NewDelegatingAuthorizationOptions(),
69: 		Audit:          NewAuditOptions(),
70: 		Features:       NewFeatureOptions(),
71: 		CoreAPI:        NewCoreAPIOptions(),
72: 		// Wired a global by default that sadly people will abuse to have different meanings in different repos.
73: 		// Please consider creating your own FeatureGate so you can have a consistent meaning for what a variable contains
74: 		// across different repos.  Future you will thank you.
75: 		FeatureGate:                feature.DefaultFeatureGate,
76: 		ExtraAdmissionInitializers: func(c *server.RecommendedConfig) ([]admission.PluginInitializer, error) { return nil, nil },
77: 		Admission:                  NewAdmissionOptions(),
78: 		EgressSelector:             NewEgressSelectorOptions(),
79: 		Traces:                     NewTracingOptions(),
80: 	}
81: }
82: 
83: func (o *RecommendedOptions) AddFlags(fs *pflag.FlagSet) {
84: 	o.Etcd.AddFlags(fs)
85: 	o.SecureServing.AddFlags(fs)
86: 	o.Authentication.AddFlags(fs)
87: 	o.Authorization.AddFlags(fs)
88: 	o.Audit.AddFlags(fs)
89: 	o.Features.AddFlags(fs)
90: 	o.CoreAPI.AddFlags(fs)
91: 	o.Admission.AddFlags(fs)
92: 	o.EgressSelector.AddFlags(fs)
93: 	o.Traces.AddFlags(fs)
94: }
95: 
96: // ApplyTo adds RecommendedOptions to the server configuration.
97: // pluginInitializers can be empty, it is only need for additional initializers.
98: func (o *RecommendedOptions) ApplyTo(config *server.RecommendedConfig) error {
99: 	if err := o.Etcd.ApplyTo(&config.Config); err != nil {
100: 		return err
101: 	}
102: 	if err := o.EgressSelector.ApplyTo(&config.Config); err != nil {
103: 		return err
104: 	}
105: 	if err := o.Traces.ApplyTo(config.Config.EgressSelector, &config.Config); err != nil {
106: 		return err
107: 	}
108: 	if err := o.SecureServing.ApplyTo(&config.Config.SecureServing, &config.Config.LoopbackClientConfig); err != nil {
109: 		return err
110: 	}
111: 	if err := o.Authentication.ApplyTo(&config.Config.Authentication, config.SecureServing, config.OpenAPIConfig); err != nil {
112: 		return err
113: 	}
114: 	if err := o.Authorization.ApplyTo(&config.Config.Authorization); err != nil {
115: 		return err
116: 	}
117: 	if err := o.Audit.ApplyTo(&config.Config); err != nil {
118: 		return err
119: 	}
120: 	if err := o.CoreAPI.ApplyTo(config); err != nil {
121: 		return err
122: 	}
123: 	var kubeClient kubernetes.Interface
124: 	var dynamicClient dynamic.Interface
125: 	if config.ClientConfig != nil {
126: 		var err error
127: 		kubeClient, err = kubernetes.NewForConfig(config.ClientConfig)
128: 		if err != nil {
129: 			return err
130: 		}
131: 		dynamicClient, err = dynamic.NewForConfig(config.ClientConfig)
132: 		if err != nil {
133: 			return err
134: 		}
135: 	}
136: 	if err := o.Features.ApplyTo(&config.Config, kubeClient, config.SharedInformerFactory); err != nil {
137: 		return err
138: 	}
139: 	initializers, err := o.ExtraAdmissionInitializers(config)
140: 	if err != nil {
141: 		return err
142: 	}
143: 	if err := o.Admission.ApplyTo(&config.Config, config.SharedInformerFactory, kubeClient, dynamicClient, o.FeatureGate,
144: 		config.EffectiveVersion,
145: 		initializers...); err != nil {
146: 		return err
147: 	}
148: 	return nil
149: }
150: 
151: func (o *RecommendedOptions) Validate() []error {
152: 	errors := []error{}
153: 	errors = append(errors, o.Etcd.Validate()...)
154: 	errors = append(errors, o.SecureServing.Validate()...)
155: 	errors = append(errors, o.Authentication.Validate()...)
156: 	errors = append(errors, o.Authorization.Validate()...)
157: 	errors = append(errors, o.Audit.Validate()...)
158: 	errors = append(errors, o.Features.Validate()...)
159: 	errors = append(errors, o.CoreAPI.Validate()...)
160: 	errors = append(errors, o.Admission.Validate()...)
161: 	errors = append(errors, o.EgressSelector.Validate()...)
162: 	errors = append(errors, o.Traces.Validate()...)
163: 
164: 	return errors
165: }

67: 	// DefaultEventFreshDuration is the default time duration of events
68: 	// we want to keep.
69: 	// We set it to defaultBookmarkFrequency plus epsilon to maximize
70: 	// chances that last bookmark was sent within kept history, at the
71: 	// same time, minimizing the needed memory usage.
72: 	DefaultEventFreshDuration = defaultBookmarkFrequency + 15*time.Second
73: 
74: 	// defaultBookmarkFrequency defines how frequently watch bookmarks should be send

   - `pkg/server/` - Core server infrastructure (GenericAPIServer, Config, handler chain)

   - `storage/etcd3/` - etcd3 implementation
   - `storage/cacher/` - in-memory watch cache (cacher.go, watch_cache.go)
   - `registry/generic/registry/Store` - generic CRUD store with strategies

   - `BuildHandlerChainFunc` - custom handler chain
   - `PostStartHookFunc` / `PreShutdownHookFunc` - lifecycle hooks
   - `admission.Interface` / `MutationInterface` / `ValidationInterface` - admission plugins
   - `rest.Storage` interfaces - custom storage backends
   - `APIGroupInfo` + `InstallAPIGroup` - new API groups
   - `RecommendedOptions` - configuration composition

8. **Lifecycle/Shutdown**:
   - `lifecycle_signals.go` - named signals for shutdown events
   - `RunWithContext` - complex shutdown sequence with multiple phases
   - `ShutdownDelayDuration` (default 70s) → drain requests → stop HTTP server

9. **Operational Implications**:
   - Watch cache reduces etcd load dramatically
   - Priority and Fairness (APF) prevents overload
   - Graceful shutdown with configurable delays
   - `MaxRequestsInFlight` / `MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight` for concurrency control

arguments.file_text:
# Kubernetes `k8s.io/apiserver` Library: Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The `k8s.io/apiserver` library (located at `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is the **generic API server framework** that underpins every Kubernetes API server process. It is not a standalone binary; it is a reusable library that provides the complete infrastructure for serving REST APIs over HTTPS, including authentication, authorization, admission control, serialization, storage abstraction, watch caching, and graceful lifecycle management.

The library's central abstraction is `GenericAPIServer` (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go`), a composable server instance that can be configured with different API groups, handler chains, and storage backends. The `kube-apiserver` binary itself is built by composing three `GenericAPIServer` instances into a delegation chain.

### Boundaries

**In scope** (what this library owns):
- HTTP serving infrastructure (TLS, mux routing, handler chain construction)
- Authentication and authorization framework interfaces and union implementations
- Admission control plugin framework and built-in plugins (webhook, CEL, namespace, resource quota)
- REST endpoint installation (`APIInstaller`) and CRUD handler dispatch
- Generic registry (`Store`) and storage abstraction (`storage.Interface`)
- Watch cache (`storage/cacher/`) that sits between the registry and etcd
- API group registration (`APIGroupInfo`, `InstallAPIGroup`)
- Server lifecycle management (startup hooks, graceful shutdown signals)
- OpenAPI/discovery endpoint generation

**Out of scope** (owned by other libraries):
- Type definitions for built-in resources → `k8s.io/api`
- Scheme, serialization, and conversion primitives → `k8s.io/apimachinery`
- CRD-specific handling → `k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver`
- Aggregation layer proxy → `k8s.io/kube-aggregator`
- Client libraries → `k8s.io/client-go`

### Upstream/Downstream Dependencies

```
k8s.io/apimachinery          ← runtime.Scheme, GVK, serialization, watch
k8s.io/api                   ← built-in type definitions
k8s.io/client-go             ← informers, REST client (for loopback and webhooks)
k8s.io/component-base        ← feature gates, metrics, tracing, version
etcd (via go.etcd.io/etcd)   ← persistent storage backend
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
              k8s.io/apiserver  (this library)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver  ← consumes GenericAPIServer for CRDs
k8s.io/kube-aggregator          ← consumes GenericAPIServer for aggregation
kubernetes/pkg/controlplane     ← wires core API groups into GenericAPIServer
```

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 `pkg/server/` — Server Core

**`config.go`** defines the two primary configuration types:

- `Config` — the complete configuration struct for a `GenericAPIServer`. Its fields cover TLS serving (`SecureServing`), authentication (`Authentication`), authorization (`Authorization`), admission (`AdmissionControl`), flow control (`FlowControl`), audit (`AuditBackend`, `AuditPolicyRuleEvaluator`), storage (`RESTOptionsGetter`, `ResourceTransformers`), and lifecycle tuning (`ShutdownDelayDuration`, `MaxRequestsInFlight`, `MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight`). The comment in the source explicitly groups fields by importance for composers.

- `RecommendedConfig` — embeds `Config` and adds `SharedInformerFactory` and `ClientConfig`, providing the standard starting point for extension API servers.

The `Config.Complete()` method (line 702) seals the configuration into a `CompletedConfig`, filling in derived fields (external address, OpenAPI version, discovery addresses, request info resolver). Only a `CompletedConfig` can call `New()` to produce a `GenericAPIServer`.

**`genericapiserver.go`** defines `GenericAPIServer` and the `DelegationTarget` interface:

```go
// DelegationTarget is an interface which allows for composition of API servers
type DelegationTarget interface {
    UnprotectedHandler() http.Handler
    PostStartHooks() map[string]postStartHookEntry
    PreShutdownHooks() map[string]preShutdownHookEntry
    HealthzChecks() []healthz.HealthChecker
    NextDelegate() DelegationTarget
    PrepareRun() preparedGenericAPIServer
    MuxAndDiscoveryCompleteSignals() map[string]<-chan struct{}
    Destroy()
}
```

This interface is what makes the three-server delegation chain possible. Each server in the chain holds a reference to the next via `delegationTarget DelegationTarget` (line 220). When a request arrives for a path that the current server does not handle, it falls through to `delegationTarget.UnprotectedHandler()`.

**`handler.go`** defines `APIServerHandler`, the HTTP routing core:

```go
type APIServerHandler struct {
    FullHandlerChain   http.Handler        // filter chain wrapping Director
    GoRestfulContainer *restful.Container  // go-restful for /api and /apis paths
    NonGoRestfulMux    *mux.PathRecorderMux // fallback for health, metrics, etc.
    Director           http.Handler        // routes between the two
}
```

The `Director.ServeHTTP` method (line 121) inspects the request path against registered go-restful `WebService` root paths. If a match is found, it dispatches to `GoRestfulContainer`; otherwise it falls through to `NonGoRestfulMux`. This dual-mux design exists because go-restful is needed for OpenAPI generation but is too strict for delegation fallthrough.

**`hooks.go`** implements `PostStartHookFunc` and `PreShutdownHookFunc`. Each `PostStartHook` is run in its own goroutine after the server starts listening. Critically, each hook is automatically wired to a `/readyz` health check (`postStartHookHealthz`) that returns failure until the hook's `done` channel is closed (line 109). This means the server will not report ready until all post-start hooks complete.

**`lifecycle_signals.go`** defines the named channel-based signals that coordinate the shutdown sequence: `ShutdownInitiated`, `AfterShutdownDelayDuration`, `NotAcceptingNewRequest`, `InFlightRequestsDrained`, `HTTPServerStoppedListening`, `PreShutdownHooksStopped`, and `MuxAndDiscoveryComplete`.

### 2.2 `pkg/endpoints/` — HTTP Endpoint Installation

**`installer.go`** defines `APIInstaller`, which takes an `APIGroupVersion` (containing a map of resource names to `rest.Storage` implementations) and registers HTTP routes in the `GoRestfulContainer`. For each resource, it introspects which `rest` interfaces the storage implements (e.g., `rest.Getter`, `rest.Creater`, `rest.Updater`, `rest.Watcher`) and registers the corresponding HTTP verbs. The `action` struct (line 69) captures the verb, path, and parameters for each registered route.

**`handlers/`** contains the per-verb HTTP handler functions:
- `create.go` — `createHandler`: reads body, decodes, runs field manager, calls admission, calls `r.Create()`
- `get.go` — handles GET (single object and list)
- `update.go` — handles PUT
- `patch.go` — handles PATCH (JSON merge, strategic merge, server-side apply)
- `delete.go` — handles DELETE
- `watch.go` — handles long-running WATCH requests

**`filters/`** contains the per-request HTTP middleware (authentication, authorization, audit, timeout, panic recovery, etc.) that are assembled into the handler chain.

**`request/`** provides context helpers for storing and retrieving per-request information (user info, namespace, request info) from `context.Context`.

### 2.3 `pkg/admission/` — Admission Control

The admission framework is defined in `interfaces.go`:

```go
type MutationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Admit(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) (err error)
}

type ValidationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Validate(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) (err error)
}
```

`Attributes` (line 31) provides the admission plugin with everything it needs: the object being mutated, the old object (for updates), the user info, the operation type, and whether it is a dry run. The `IsDryRun()` method (line 52) is critical: plugins with external side effects must check this flag to avoid acting on dry-run requests.

`chain.go` implements `chainAdmissionHandler`, a slice of `Interface` values. `Admit` iterates the chain and calls each mutator in order, returning immediately on the first error. `Validate` does the same for validators. This means **mutation always runs before validation** — the two passes are separate.

`plugins/` contains built-in plugins:
- `webhook/` — MutatingAdmissionWebhook and ValidatingAdmissionWebhook
- `cel/` — ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL-based policy engine)
- `namespace/` — NamespaceLifecycle (prevents operations in terminating namespaces)
- `resourcequota/` — ResourceQuota enforcement
- `policy/` — PodSecurity admission

### 2.4 `pkg/registry/` — REST Storage Abstractions

**`registry/rest/rest.go`** defines the storage interface hierarchy. The key interfaces are:
- `rest.Storage` — base interface with `Destroy()`
- `rest.Getter` — `Get(ctx, name, options)`
- `rest.Lister` — `List(ctx, options)`
- `rest.Creater` — `Create(ctx, name, obj, createValidation, options)`
- `rest.Updater` — `Update(ctx, name, objInfo, createValidation, updateValidation, forceAllowCreate, options)`
- `rest.GracefulDeleter` — `Delete(ctx, name, deleteValidation, options)`
- `rest.Watcher` — `Watch(ctx, options)`
- `rest.StandardStorage` — aggregates all of the above

**`registry/generic/registry/store.go`** provides `Store`, the generic implementation of `StandardStorage`. It is designed to be embedded in resource-specific storage types. Its key fields are:

```go
type Store struct {
    NewFunc         func() runtime.Object
    NewListFunc     func() runtime.Object
    KeyRootFunc     func(ctx context.Context) string
    KeyFunc         func(ctx context.Context, name string) (string, error)
    CreateStrategy  rest.RESTCreateStrategy
    UpdateStrategy  rest.RESTUpdateStrategy
    DeleteStrategy  rest.RESTDeleteStrategy
    Storage         DryRunnableStorage
    // ... hooks: BeginCreate, AfterCreate, BeginUpdate, AfterUpdate, AfterDelete
}
```

The `Strategy` objects (`RESTCreateStrategy`, `RESTUpdateStrategy`, `RESTDeleteStrategy`) are where resource-specific business logic lives: field defaulting, validation, name generation, and immutability enforcement. The `Store` calls into these strategies at the appropriate points in each CRUD operation.

**`registry/generic/storage_decorator.go`** defines `StorageDecorator`, a function type that wraps a raw `storage.Interface` (typically the etcd3 client) with the watch cache. The default decorator used in production is `generic.UndecoratedStorage` for the event resource and the cacher-wrapped version for everything else.

### 2.5 `pkg/storage/` — Storage Layer

**`storage/interfaces.go`** defines `storage.Interface`, the abstract key-value store contract:
- `Create`, `Delete`, `Watch`, `WatchList`, `Get`, `GetList`, `GuaranteedUpdate`
- `Versioner` — maps between etcd `mod_revision` and Kubernetes `resourceVersion`

**`storage/etcd3/`** — the concrete etcd3 implementation of `storage.Interface`.

**`storage/cacher/cacher.go`** — the in-memory watch cache. It wraps a `storage.Interface` and maintains a `watchCache` (a ring buffer of recent events) plus a `store` (a snapshot of all current objects). The cacher serves most `List` and `Watch` requests from memory, falling back to etcd only when the cache's event buffer is insufficient.

**`storage/value/`** — the encryption transformer layer. `ResourceTransformers` in `Config` plugs in here, enabling at-rest encryption of etcd values.

### 2.6 `pkg/server/options/` — Configuration Composition

`RecommendedOptions` (`options/recommended.go`) is the standard entry point for building an extension API server. It bundles:

```go
type RecommendedOptions struct {
    Etcd           *EtcdOptions
    SecureServing  *SecureServingOptionsWithLoopback
    Authentication *DelegatingAuthenticationOptions
    Authorization  *DelegatingAuthorizationOptions
    Audit          *AuditOptions
    Features       *FeatureOptions
    CoreAPI        *CoreAPIOptions
    Admission      *AdmissionOptions
    EgressSelector *EgressSelectorOptions
    Traces         *TracingOptions
}
```

`ApplyTo(config *server.RecommendedConfig)` applies each option group to the config in dependency order (etcd → egress → TLS → auth → audit → features → admission). This is the canonical way to configure an aggregated API server.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

### 3.1 Server Chain Routing

When `kube-apiserver` receives a request, it first enters the **Aggregator Server** (built by `kube-aggregator`). The aggregator checks whether an `APIService` object has claimed the request's group/version path. If so, it proxies the request to the backing extension API server. If not, it delegates to the **Kube API Server** (core), which handles built-in groups (`core/v1`, `apps/v1`, etc.). If the core server cannot handle the path, it delegates to the **API Extensions Server** (`apiextensions-apiserver`), which handles CRD-backed resources. If none of the three servers match, the `emptyDelegate` at the end of the chain returns a 404.

The delegation mechanism is implemented via `DelegationTarget.UnprotectedHandler()`. In `completedConfig.New()` (line 803 of `config.go`):

```go
apiServerHandler := NewAPIServerHandler(name, c.Serializer, handlerChainBuilder,
    delegationTarget.UnprotectedHandler())
```

The `delegationTarget.UnprotectedHandler()` becomes the `notFoundHandler` of the current server's `NonGoRestfulMux`. When no route matches, the mux calls this handler, which is the next server in the chain.

### 3.2 Handler Chain (Filter Pipeline)

Every request that enters a `GenericAPIServer` passes through `FullHandlerChain`, which is built by `BuildHandlerChainFunc` (defaulting to `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` in `config.go`). The filters are applied in reverse order of wrapping (outermost first):

1. **Panic recovery** — catches panics and returns 500
2. **Request timeout** — enforces `RequestTimeout` for non-long-running requests
3. **CORS** — adds CORS headers if configured
4. **HSTS** — adds HSTS headers
5. **Audit** — begins audit event recording for the request
6. **Authentication** — calls the union authenticator; populates `user.Info` in context. The body is **not yet read**.
7. **Impersonation** — checks and applies impersonation headers
8. **Authorization** — calls the union authorizer with the user info and request attributes. Still no body deserialization.
9. **Priority and Fairness** — classifies the request into a `FlowSchema` and `PriorityLevel`, queues or rejects based on concurrency limits
10. **Wait group tracking** — registers the request with `NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup` or `WatchRequestWaitGroup` for graceful shutdown
11. **Director** → `GoRestfulContainer` or `NonGoRestfulMux`

### 3.3 REST Handler Dispatch (CREATE example)

Once the filter chain passes the request to the go-restful container, the `APIInstaller`-registered handler for the resource and verb is invoked. For a `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments`, the `createHandler` function in `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` executes:

1. **Extract namespace/name** from URL via `scope.Namer.Name(req)` (line 61)
2. **Negotiate output media type** — determines response format (JSON, YAML, Protobuf)
3. **Read body** — `limitedReadBodyWithRecordMetric` enforces `MaxRequestBodyBytes` (line 94). This is the **first point** where the request body is consumed.
4. **Decode** — `decoder.Decode(body, &defaultGVK, original)` converts the wire bytes to the internal hub version via the `Scheme`'s conversion functions (line 127)
5. **Field validation** — if `fieldValidation=Strict`, uses a strict decoder that rejects unknown fields
6. **Wipe system fields** — `rest.WipeObjectMetaSystemFields` clears server-managed fields like `resourceVersion`, `uid`, `creationTimestamp` from the incoming object (line 171)
7. **Field manager update** — `scope.FieldManager.UpdateNoErrors` applies server-side apply field ownership tracking (line 199)
8. **Admission** — `finisher.FinishRequest` calls the admission chain:
   - First `Admit` (mutation pass) — all `MutationInterface` plugins run in order
   - Then `Validate` (validation pass) — all `ValidationInterface` plugins run in order
   - The `CreateStrategy.Validate` is called inside `r.Create()` as `createValidation`
9. **Store** — `r.Create(ctx, name, obj, createValidation, options)` calls `Store.Create`, which:
   - Calls `CreateStrategy.PrepareForCreate` (sets defaults, clears status)
   - Calls `CreateStrategy.Validate` (business logic validation)
   - Calls `storage.Interface.Create` → etcd3 client → etcd
10. **Serialize response** — the stored object is converted back to the requested version and serialized

### 3.4 Watch Cache Flow

For `GET /apis/apps/v1/deployments?watch=true`, the request reaches `Store.Watch`, which calls `storage.Interface.Watch`. In production, this `storage.Interface` is the `Cacher` (from `storage/cacher/cacher.go`), not the raw etcd client.

The `Cacher` maintains:
- A `watchCache` ring buffer (default ~75 seconds of events, controlled by `DefaultEventFreshDuration = defaultBookmarkFrequency + 15s`)
- A `store` snapshot of all current objects (populated via an initial LIST from etcd)
- A background goroutine that watches etcd and feeds events into the ring buffer

When a watch request arrives, the cacher creates a `cacheWatcher` and registers it. The cacher dispatches events from its ring buffer to all registered watchers. If a watcher's requested `resourceVersion` is older than the ring buffer's oldest event, the cacher falls back to a direct etcd watch for that watcher.

For consistent reads (LIST with `resourceVersion=`), the cacher fetches the latest `mod_revision` from etcd, waits until its internal cache has caught up to that revision, then serves the list from memory — avoiding a full etcd scan.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Concurrency Limits

`Config` exposes two hard concurrency limits:
- `MaxRequestsInFlight` — maximum parallel non-mutating requests (default 400)
- `MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight` — maximum parallel mutating requests (default 200)

These are enforced by the `WithMaxInFlightLimit` filter in the handler chain. When the limit is reached, new requests receive a `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header. These limits are a blunt instrument; the **Priority and Fairness** (APF) subsystem (`pkg/util/flowcontrol/`) provides finer-grained control via `FlowSchema` and `PriorityLevelConfiguration` objects, classifying requests by user, verb, and resource into queues with configurable concurrency shares.

### 4.2 Watch Cache Tradeoffs

The watch cache dramatically reduces etcd read load — in a large cluster, thousands of controllers each watching thousands of objects would overwhelm etcd without it. However:

- **Memory pressure**: The cache holds all objects of each resource type in memory. A cluster with millions of events or very large objects can exhaust apiserver memory.
- **Stale reads**: By default, LIST requests are served from cache. A client that needs a guaranteed-fresh read must use `resourceVersion=0` (served from cache) or `resourceVersion=""` (consistent read, which still uses the cache but waits for it to catch up to the latest etcd revision).
- **Ring buffer overflow**: If the cacher's event ring buffer fills up (because a watcher is too slow), the watcher is forcibly closed and must re-list. This is logged as a "watch buffer full" event.
- **Bookmark events**: The cacher sends periodic bookmark events (every `defaultBookmarkFrequency = 1 minute`) to advance watchers' `resourceVersion` even when no objects change, preventing expensive re-lists.

### 4.3 Optimistic Concurrency

The `resourceVersion` field on every Kubernetes object maps directly to etcd's `mod_revision`. When a client submits a PUT or PATCH, the apiserver passes the client's `resourceVersion` as a precondition to `storage.Interface.GuaranteedUpdate`. If the etcd `mod_revision` has advanced (another writer modified the object), etcd rejects the write and the apiserver returns `409 Conflict` with the message defined in `store.go`:

```go
const OptimisticLockErrorMsg = "the object has been modified; please apply your changes to the latest version and try again"
```

This forces clients into a read-modify-write loop. Server-Side Apply (`managedFields`) is an alternative that avoids this by tracking field ownership, allowing multiple managers to update disjoint fields without conflicts.

### 4.4 Admission Plugin Ordering and Re-invocation

Mutation plugins run before validation plugins. Within each pass, plugins run in the order they are registered. If a mutating webhook modifies an object, other mutating webhooks that have already run may need to re-run to account for the change. The `ReinvocationContext` interface (in `admission/interfaces.go`) and `reinvocation.go` implement this re-invocation policy: a plugin can call `SetShouldReinvoke()` to signal that the chain should be re-run.

This creates a potential for infinite loops if two plugins keep triggering each other's re-invocation. The framework limits re-invocation to a single additional pass.

### 4.5 Graceful Shutdown Complexity

The shutdown sequence in `RunWithContext` (`genericapiserver.go`, lines 525–700+) is deliberately multi-phase to avoid dropping in-flight requests when a load balancer is still routing traffic to the server:

```
T0:       SIGTERM received → ShutdownInitiated → /readyz returns failure
T0+70s:   AfterShutdownDelayDuration → PreShutdownHooks run
          → NotAcceptingNewRequest
          → NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup.Wait() (up to 60s)
          → WatchRequestWaitGroup.Wait() (if ShutdownWatchTerminationGracePeriod > 0)
          → InFlightRequestsDrained
          → HTTP server stops listening
          → AuditBackend.Shutdown()
```

The 70-second `ShutdownDelayDuration` default gives load balancers time to detect the failing `/readyz` and stop routing new traffic. Operators running in environments with faster load balancer convergence can reduce this value.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 Custom Handler Chain (`BuildHandlerChainFunc`)

`Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (line 186 of `config.go`) is a function field:

```go
BuildHandlerChainFunc func(apiHandler http.Handler, c *Config) (secure http.Handler)
```

Replacing this function allows complete control over the filter pipeline. The default is `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`. Extension API servers built with `RecommendedOptions` typically use the default.

**Risk**: Replacing the handler chain incorrectly can bypass authentication or authorization. Any custom chain must call the inner `apiHandler` and must not skip the authentication/authorization filters unless the server is intentionally unauthenticated (e.g., a metrics-only endpoint).

### 5.2 Admission Plugins

New admission plugins implement `MutationInterface` and/or `ValidationInterface` from `pkg/admission/interfaces.go`. They are registered via `admission.RegisterPlugin` and initialized via `PluginInitializer.Initialize`. The `RecommendedOptions.Admission.ApplyTo` call wires them into the chain.

**Risk**: Mutating admission plugins that modify objects must be idempotent (re-invocation policy) and must handle `IsDryRun()` correctly. A plugin that makes external API calls on every admission request can become a latency bottleneck for the entire cluster. Webhook-based plugins add network round-trips and can cause cascading failures if the webhook endpoint is unavailable (mitigated by `failurePolicy: Ignore`).

### 5.3 PostStartHooks and PreShutdownHooks

`Config.AddPostStartHook(name string, hook PostStartHookFunc)` registers a function to run after the server starts listening. The hook receives a `PostStartHookContext` with a `LoopbackClientConfig` for privileged in-process API access and a `context.Context` that is cancelled when the server stops.

```go
type PostStartHookFunc func(context PostStartHookContext) error
```

Each hook is automatically wired to a `/readyz` health check. If the hook has not completed, `/readyz` returns failure. If the hook returns a non-nil error, `klog.Fatalf` is called — the server crashes.

**Risk**: A hook that blocks indefinitely will prevent the server from ever becoming ready. A hook that panics will crash the server. Hooks should be designed as mini-controllers that handle partial completion and restarts gracefully (the comment in `hooks.go` explicitly calls this out).

### 5.4 Custom REST Storage (`rest.Storage` Interfaces)

To add a new resource, implement the appropriate `rest` interfaces from `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` and register them via `APIGroupInfo.VersionedResourcesStorageMap`. The `genericregistry.Store` can be embedded to get standard CRUD behavior, with customization via `Strategy` objects.

**Risk**: Implementing `rest.Updater` without implementing `rest.Creater` (or vice versa) can lead to unexpected behavior when the `APIInstaller` introspects the storage. The `Store` type assertion checks (`var _ rest.StandardStorage = &Store{}`) in `store.go` (line 252) serve as compile-time guards for the built-in implementation but not for custom implementations.

### 5.5 New API Groups (`InstallAPIGroup`)

`GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroup(apiGroupInfo *APIGroupInfo)` registers a new API group. The `APIGroupInfo` struct bundles the `Scheme`, `NegotiatedSerializer`, `ParameterCodec`, and `VersionedResourcesStorageMap`. The `APIInstaller` then registers HTTP routes for each resource.

**Risk**: Registering two API groups with overlapping paths causes a panic in the go-restful container. The `PrioritizedVersions` field in `APIGroupInfo` controls which version is served at the group-level discovery endpoint; getting this wrong can cause clients to use a deprecated version.

### 5.6 Storage Encryption (`ResourceTransformers`)

`Config.ResourceTransformers` (line 222) plugs in at-rest encryption. The `storage/value/` package defines the `Transformer` interface. The `encryptionconfig/` package in `server/options/` provides the standard KMS-based implementation.

**Risk**: Changing the encryption key or provider without a proper migration procedure will make existing etcd data unreadable. The `StorageVersionManager` tracks which storage version each resource is persisted in, enabling migration.

### 5.7 Feature Gates

`Config.FeatureGate` (line 169) controls which features are active. Feature gates are checked at runtime via `utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate.Enabled(feature)`. The `EffectiveVersion` field (line 157) enables version emulation — running a newer binary but behaving like an older version for API availability.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**Watch cache sizing**: The watch cache ring buffer size is determined by `DefaultEventFreshDuration` (approximately 75 seconds of events). In clusters with high write rates, this buffer can fill quickly, causing watchers to be evicted and forced to re-list. Monitor the `apiserver_watch_cache_events_dispatched_total` and `apiserver_watch_events_dropped_total` metrics.

**Admission webhook latency**: Each mutating or validating webhook adds a synchronous network round-trip to every write request. A webhook with p99 latency of 100ms will add 100ms to every `kubectl apply`. Use `timeoutSeconds` in `WebhookConfiguration` to bound this, and set `failurePolicy: Ignore` for non-critical webhooks.

**Priority and Fairness**: APF (`pkg/util/flowcontrol/`) replaces the blunt `MaxRequestsInFlight` limits with per-flow queuing. Misconfigured `FlowSchema` objects can starve critical system components (e.g., the node lifecycle controller) if they are placed in a low-priority level. Monitor `apiserver_flowcontrol_rejected_requests_total` and `apiserver_flowcontrol_request_wait_duration_seconds`.

**Serialization cost**: The apiserver serializes every response. For large LIST responses (e.g., listing all pods in a large cluster), this can be CPU-intensive. The `Protobuf` content type is significantly faster than JSON for large responses; `kubectl` uses JSON by default but internal components use Protobuf.

### 6.2 Reliability

**etcd dependency**: The apiserver is completely dependent on etcd for writes and consistent reads. An etcd partition or leader election will cause write failures. The watch cache provides read availability during brief etcd disruptions (existing watchers continue to receive events from the cache), but new consistent reads will fail.

**Graceful shutdown**: The multi-phase shutdown sequence (Section 4.5) is designed to avoid dropping requests during rolling updates. Operators must ensure `ShutdownDelayDuration` is longer than the load balancer's health check interval. If the load balancer removes the server from rotation before `ShutdownDelayDuration` elapses, the delay is wasted; if it is too short, requests will be dropped.

**PostStartHook failures**: A failing `PostStartHook` calls `klog.Fatalf`, crashing the server. This is intentional — a hook that cannot complete its initialization (e.g., cannot connect to etcd to install CRDs) should not allow the server to serve traffic in a partially-initialized state. However, it means a bug in a hook can prevent the server from ever starting.

### 6.3 Maintainability

**Code generation dependency**: The apiserver framework relies heavily on generated code (`deepcopy-gen`, `conversion-gen`, `defaulter-gen`, `openapi-gen`). Adding a new field to an API type requires re-running the generators. Forgetting to regenerate will cause runtime panics (missing `DeepCopy` methods) or incorrect behavior (missing conversion functions).

**Strategy pattern**: The separation of generic CRUD logic (`Store`) from resource-specific logic (`Strategy`) is the primary maintainability mechanism. New resources should implement their business logic in a `Strategy` object and embed `Store`, rather than reimplementing CRUD from scratch. This ensures consistent behavior for features like dry-run, server-side apply, and optimistic concurrency.

**Delegation chain complexity**: The three-server delegation chain makes it difficult to trace which server handles a given request path. The `muxAndDiscoveryCompleteSignals` mechanism (line 277 of `genericapiserver.go`) exists specifically to prevent 404 responses during startup when routes are still being registered across the chain.

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

| Path | Role |
|------|------|
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Official architecture overview (primary reference) |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go` | `Config`, `RecommendedConfig`, `CompletedConfig`, `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`, `BuildHandlerChainFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | `GenericAPIServer`, `APIGroupInfo`, `DelegationTarget`, `PrepareRun`, `RunWithContext`, `InstallAPIGroup` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/handler.go` | `APIServerHandler`, `Director`, dual-mux routing |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/hooks.go` | `PostStartHookFunc`, `PreShutdownHookFunc`, `RunPostStartHooks`, `postStartHookHealthz` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go` | Named shutdown signals (`ShutdownInitiated`, `InFlightRequestsDrained`, etc.) |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/recommended.go` | `RecommendedOptions`, `ApplyTo` — standard extension server configuration |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/etcd.go` | `EtcdOptions` — storage backend configuration |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/encryptionconfig/` | At-rest encryption configuration |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | `APIInstaller`, route registration, `ConvertGroupVersionIntoToDiscovery` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | `createHandler` — full CREATE request lifecycle |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go` | PATCH handling including server-side apply |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/watch.go` | Long-running WATCH handler |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/` | Per-request HTTP middleware (auth, audit, timeout, etc.) |

| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, `Attributes`, `PluginInitializer` |

| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/reinvocation.go` | Mutating webhook re-invocation policy |
